title: Overview - Ontology - Palantir
summary: The Palantir Ontology is an operational layer that sits on top of digital assets (datasets, virtual tables, models) and connects them to real-world counterparts. It serves as a digital twin containing semantic elements (objects, properties, links) and kinetic elements (actions, functions, dynamic security). The Ontology maps data to meaningful business concepts rather than raw tables and columns.
relevantParts: Defines the Ontology as operational layer connecting data to real-world entities
Introduces semantic vs kinetic distinction in Ontology elements
Core reference for all Ontology primitives and their relationships

title: Core concepts - Palantir
summary: Defines the fundamental building blocks of the Ontology: object types (schema definition of real-world entities), properties (characteristics), link types (relationships), and action types (mutations). An object type is analogous to a dataset, an object to a row, and a property to a column. The Ontology creates a complete picture by mapping datasets and models to these primitives.
relevantParts: Formal definitions of all core Ontology primitives
Analogies between Ontology concepts and traditional data structures
Explains how datasets map to Ontology types

title: Object and link types - Object types - Overview - Palantir
summary: An object type is the schema definition of a real-world entity or event. An object instance refers to a single instance of an object type corresponding to a single real-world entity. To create objects, organizations add backing datasources to the object type and connect their enterprise data into the Ontology. Properties define characteristics, and shared properties allow consistent modeling across types.
relevantParts: Detailed definition of Object Type as schema for real-world entities
Relationship between object types and backing datasources
Introduction of shared properties concept

title: Object and link types - Properties - Overview - Palantir
summary: A property of an object type is the schema definition of a characteristic of a real-world entity or event. Properties are analogous to columns in a dataset, and property values are analogous to fields. Properties have metadata including base type, render hints, visibility settings, title key (display name), and primary key (unique identifier).
relevantParts: Formal property definition with dataset/column analogy
Property metadata: base type, render hints, visibility, keys
Distinction between title key and primary key

title: Object and link types - Properties - Base types - Palantir
summary: Documents the available property base types in the Ontology including standard types (string, boolean, integer, etc.) and specialized types like Timestamp, Geohash/Geopoint, Geoshape, Attachment, and Time Series. The base type determines the set of operations available in user applications and affects indexing behavior.
relevantParts: Complete reference of available property base types
Specialized types: geospatial, time series, attachments
Base type determines available operations in applications

title: Object and link types - Types reference - Palantir
summary: Comprehensive type reference for all Ontology types including object types, link types, properties, interfaces, and their metadata. Serves as the canonical reference for how types are defined, their relationships, and their API representations.
relevantParts: Complete type system reference for all Ontology primitives
API representations of types
Type relationships and composition rules

title: Object and link types - Link types - Overview - Palantir
summary: A link type is the schema definition of a relationship between two object types. Supports cardinalities: one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many. Foreign keys define one-to-one/many-to-one links, while many-to-many requires a separate datasource containing link combinations. Each side of a link type has its own display name.
relevantParts: Link type cardinality options and their semantics
Foreign key vs datasource-backed link implementation
Link type metadata including bidirectional display names

title: Object and link types - Link types - Create a link type - Palantir
summary: Step-by-step guide for creating link types in the Ontology Manager. Covers selecting the two object types to link, choosing cardinality, configuring foreign key relationships for one-to-one/many-to-one links, and selecting datasources for many-to-many links.
relevantParts: Practical guide for creating link types
Configuration differences by cardinality type
Foreign key and datasource selection process

title: Action types - Overview - Palantir
summary: An action type defines a set of changes or edits to objects, property values, and links that a user can take at once. Changes are committed atomically to the Ontology and reflected across all applications. Action types include rules (Ontology edits), parameters (user inputs), submission criteria (validation), and side effects (notifications, webhooks).
relevantParts: Action types as atomic mutation definitions
Composition of actions: rules, parameters, submission criteria, side effects
Actions as the kinetic layer of the Ontology

title: Action types - Rules - Palantir
summary: Rules define the logic of an action type, transforming parameters into Ontology edits or effects. Ontology rules include create object, modify object, delete object, add/remove links, and function rules. Function rules reference Ontology edit functions and preclude other rules since function code handles everything. Rules can also operate on interfaces for polymorphic actions.
relevantParts: Detailed rule types: create, modify, delete objects and links
Function rules as code-based alternatives to declarative rules
Interface-based rules for polymorphic operations

title: Action types - Parameters - Overview - Palantir
summary: Action type parameters define the user inputs required when taking an action. Parameters can reference object types, properties, and other Ontology elements. Each rule property is mapped to a parameter value by default, and parameter dropdowns can be filtered to restrict available options.
relevantParts: Parameter system for user-facing action inputs
Mapping between parameters and rule properties
Dropdown filtering for parameter values

title: Action types - Submission criteria - Palantir
summary: Submission criteria define validation rules that determine whether an action request is VALID or INVALID. Validation evaluates parameters and criteria but does not check existing objects or data in Foundry for performance reasons (e.g., uniqueness of primary key is not checked at validation time).
relevantParts: Validation system for action submissions
Performance trade-offs in validation (no object existence checks)
VALID/INVALID response structure

title: Action types - Function-backed actions - Overview - Palantir
summary: Function-backed actions use code (TypeScript/Python functions) to define action logic instead of declarative rules. When a function rule is present, no other rule may be configured since function code alone handles everything. This provides maximum flexibility for complex business logic in mutations.
relevantParts: Function-backed actions as alternative to declarative rules
Exclusive nature of function rules (replaces all other rules)
Code-based approach for complex action logic

title: Action types - Actions on interfaces - Palantir
summary: Actions can operate on interfaces for polymorphic behavior. Interface-based rules include: create object(s) of interface, modify object(s) of interface, and delete object(s) of interface. This allows a single action type to work across any object type implementing a given interface.
relevantParts: Polymorphic action types via interfaces
Create/modify/delete operations on interface implementations
Interface as abstraction layer for action types

title: Action types - Side effects - Overview - Palantir
summary: Action types support side effects beyond Ontology edits: notifications allow flexible user notification when an action is applied, and webhooks connect to systems outside Foundry including REST APIs and ERP systems. Side effects are triggered after the primary action rules execute.
relevantParts: Notification and webhook side effects
Integration with external systems via webhooks
Side effects as extensions to action rules

title: Interfaces - Overview - Palantir
summary: An interface is an Ontology type describing the shape and capabilities of object types, enabling polymorphism. Interfaces are composed of shared properties, link type constraints, and metadata. Unlike concrete object types, interfaces are abstract (no backing datasets, cannot be instantiated directly). Multiple object types can implement an interface, and interfaces can extend other interfaces.
relevantParts: Interface definition as abstract shape descriptor
Composition: shared properties + link type constraints + metadata
Key distinction: concrete object types vs abstract interfaces

title: Interfaces - Implement an interface - Palantir
summary: Describes how object types implement interfaces, analogous to implementing interfaces in programming languages. Implementation requires mapping shared properties to object type properties and satisfying link type constraints. Allows application code to interact with any implementing objects through the interface definition as an API layer.
relevantParts: Implementation mechanics: property mapping, constraint satisfaction
Interface as API layer for applications
Programming language interface analogy

title: Interfaces - Create an interface - Palantir
summary: Guide for creating interfaces in the Ontology Manager, including defining shared properties, configuring link type constraints, and setting metadata. Covers the process of building abstract type definitions that multiple object types can implement.
relevantParts: Step-by-step interface creation process
Configuration of shared properties and link constraints
Ontology Manager interface creation workflow

title: Interfaces - Extend an interface - Palantir
summary: Interfaces support inheritance: you can extend an interface to create a child interface that inherits properties of the parent, then add new specific properties to the child. This enables hierarchical type modeling similar to class inheritance in OOP.
relevantParts: Interface inheritance/extension mechanism
Child interfaces inherit parent shared properties
Hierarchical type modeling capability

title: Interfaces - Interface link types - Palantir
summary: Link type constraints define parameters for interface link types. Constraints specify: link target type (interface or object type), target entity, cardinality (ONE or MANY), and whether the link is required. All implementing object types must have a link satisfying these constraints if the link is required.
relevantParts: Interface link type constraint system
Cardinality options: ONE (1:1) and MANY (1:N)
Required vs optional link constraints on implementing types

title: Object and link types - Shared properties - Overview - Palantir
summary: A shared property is a property that can be used on multiple object types, enabling consistent data modeling and centralized metadata management. When associated with a shared property, direct edits to inherited metadata are disabled on the object type. Shared properties are foundational to interfaces.
relevantParts: Shared property definition and purpose
Centralized metadata management across object types
Relationship between shared properties and interfaces

title: Object and link types - Shared properties - Use shared properties on object types - Palantir
summary: When using a shared property on an object type, the property ID and API name remain unchanged to avoid breaking downstream workflows. While associated with a shared property, direct edits to metadata inherited from the shared property are disabled on the object type.
relevantParts: Backward compatibility when adopting shared properties
Metadata inheritance and edit restrictions
Practical usage patterns for shared properties

title: Object and link types - Value types - Overview - Palantir
summary: Value types are semantic wrappers around field types that include metadata and constraints, enhancing type safety and expressiveness. They capture domain-specific meaning (e.g., 'email' value type with regex constraint). Value types are scoped to a space and can be reused across object types and pipelines within that space.
relevantParts: Value types as semantic wrappers with constraints
Domain-specific validation (e.g., email regex)
Space-scoped reuse across object types and pipelines

title: Object and link types - Value types - Value type constraints - Palantir
summary: Documents the constraint system for value types, including regex patterns, range constraints, and other validation rules that enforce data quality. Constraints defined on a value type are automatically applied wherever that value type is used.
relevantParts: Constraint types available for value types
Automatic constraint propagation to all uses
Data quality enforcement via type system

title: Object and link types - Structs - Overview - Palantir
summary: A struct is an Ontology property base type allowing schema-based properties with multiple fields. Struct properties are created from struct type dataset columns. Nested structs are not supported. Structs are supported across Ontology Manager, Actions, Pipeline Builder, Workshop, and the OSDK.
relevantParts: Struct as composite property type with multiple fields
No nested struct support (flat only)
Cross-application support for struct types

title: Object and link types - Structs - Structs and shared properties - Palantir
summary: Struct properties can be used by both local and shared property types. This enables complex composite types to be shared and reused across multiple object types through the shared property mechanism.
relevantParts: Struct compatibility with shared properties
Reuse of complex composite types across object types

title: Object Views - Overview - Palantir
summary: Object Views are customizable, reusable representations of object data serving as a central hub for all information related to an object. Two types exist: Full Object Views (comprehensive overview) and Panel Object Views (focused on critical data for specific workflows). Views include property data, object links, and related applications.
relevantParts: Object Views as reusable data representations
Full vs Panel Object View distinction
Views compose property data, links, and applications

title: Functions - Functions on objects - Object sets - Palantir
summary: An object set represents an unordered collection of objects of a single type. Functions APIs support filtering (phrase, phrasePrefix, matchAllTokens), ordering (orderByRelevance), and retrieval (take, takeAsync). Filtering only works on properties with the Searchable render hint enabled. Supports Search Arounds to traverse link types.
relevantParts: ObjectSet as unordered collection of single-type objects
Filter methods: phrase, phrasePrefix, matchAllTokens, fuzzyMatchAllTokens
Searchable render hint required for filtering/ordering/aggregation

title: Functions - Overview - Palantir
summary: A function is code-based logic that takes input parameters and returns output, natively integrated with the Ontology. Functions can take objects and object sets as input, read property values, and be used across action types and applications. Queries are read-only subsets of functions exposed through the API gateway with no side effects.
relevantParts: Functions as code-based Ontology logic
Native integration with objects and object sets
Query functions as read-only subset of functions

title: Functions - Publish and call query functions through API gateway - Palantir
summary: Query types represent logic that can be run in the Ontology, created using Query Functions. Queries are read-only, cannot have side effects, and can be exposed through the API gateway for external consumption. Queries can be searched and managed in Ontology Manager.
relevantParts: Query functions as API gateway-exposed read logic
Read-only constraint (no side effects)
Management through Ontology Manager

title: Functions - TypeScript v2 - Ontology edits - Palantir
summary: TypeScript v2 functions can perform Ontology edits using the Edits type and createEditBatch from @osdk/functions. Functions must explicitly declare edited entities. Edits include Object and Link types. Struct properties can be edited using TypeScript interfaces matching struct fields. Function-backed Actions provide permissions and metadata.
relevantParts: TypeScript v2 edit API: Edits type, createEditBatch
Explicit declaration of edited entities
Struct property editing via TypeScript interfaces

title: Functions - Functions on objects - Objects and links - Palantir
summary: Describes how functions interact with objects and links in the Ontology. Properties of each object type are converted to fields on generated TypeScript interfaces. Links can be traversed as ObjectSets to avoid loading linked objects into memory. Search Around APIs are generated for linked object sets.
relevantParts: TypeScript interface generation for object types
Link traversal via ObjectSet (lazy loading)
Search Around API generation for links

title: Ontology SDK - Overview - Palantir
summary: OSDKs are specialized generated libraries providing intuitive interfaces for the Ontology. Supports NPM (TypeScript), Pip/Conda (Python), Maven (Java), and OpenAPI. Generated types and functions are based on the relevant Ontology subset. Enables accelerated development with ergonomic access to Ontology APIs for reading and writing.
relevantParts: OSDK as generated type-safe library per Ontology
Multi-language support: TypeScript, Python, Java, OpenAPI
Subset-based generation for relevant Ontology entities

title: GitHub - palantir/osdk-ts: Typescript related OSDK libraries
summary: Official open-source TypeScript OSDK repository. OSDK 2.0 scales linearly with Ontology shape/metadata rather than full Ontology size (unlike 1.x). Contains generated code for object types, actions, and queries. Properties are converted to TypeScript interface fields using API names from the Ontology.
relevantParts: Open source TypeScript OSDK implementation
OSDK 2.0 vs 1.x scaling differences
Generated TypeScript interfaces from Ontology types

title: Models in the Ontology - Palantir
summary: Describes how machine learning and AI models integrate with the Ontology. Models can take objects and object sets as input, read property values, and produce outputs that feed back into the Ontology. Modeling efforts build on each other over time, reducing duplicated work across use cases.
relevantParts: ML/AI model integration with Ontology primitives
Models as consumers and producers of Ontology data
Compounding value of shared modeling efforts

title: Derived properties - Palantir
summary: Derived properties are calculated at runtime based on other properties or links. Types include linked property/aggregation (from 1:1 or 1:N links) and column math (combining multiple properties). Computed in real time, they may impact module loading times. Currently a beta feature with expanding capabilities.
relevantParts: Runtime-computed properties from other properties or links
Two types: linked property/aggregation and column math
Performance implications of real-time computation

title: Object and link types - Metadata - Statuses - Palantir
summary: Every Ontology resource has a developmental status: active, experimental, deprecated, or example. Object types also support 'endorsed' status (beta) for core trusted resources. Status changes cascade: changing an object type from active to experimental marks all its properties experimental too.
relevantParts: Lifecycle status system: active, experimental, deprecated, example, endorsed
Status cascade from object type to child properties
Endorsed status for vetted core object types

title: Object and link types - Metadata - Type classes - Palantir
summary: Type classes define additional metadata for properties, link types, and action types, interpreted by user applications. They affect rendering, indexing, and application behavior. Configuration is moving to a Capabilities page in Ontology Manager. Analyzer type class affects indexing behavior specifically.
relevantParts: Type classes as extensible metadata for Ontology types
Impact on rendering and indexing behavior
Migration from type classes to Capabilities page

title: Object and link types - Metadata - Render hints - Palantir
summary: Render hints communicate information about Ontology property usage to Object Storage and user applications. Examples include sortable (enables sorting), searchable (enables filtering/aggregation), and visibility (controls display prominence). Some render hints require reindexing to take effect, affecting performance.
relevantParts: Render hints as metadata controlling application behavior
Searchable hint required for filtering/ordering/aggregation
Performance implications: some hints require reindexing

title: Object and link types - Properties - Required properties - Palantir
summary: Required properties are only supported by Object Storage V2. Can be configured to allow empty arrays (rejects null but accepts empty arrays). Useful for mandatory control properties that must always have a value. Enforces data quality at the schema level.
relevantParts: Required property support only in Object Storage V2
Configuration for empty array allowance
Schema-level data quality enforcement

title: Object and link types - Object type groups - Palantir
summary: Object type groups are classification primitives helping users search and explore their ontology. Groups are labels created and managed in Ontology Manager by ontology owners/editors. Object types can belong to multiple groups, providing organizational structure for large ontologies.
relevantParts: Groups as organizational labels for object types
Multi-group membership for object types
Discovery and navigation aid for large ontologies

title: Object and link types - Object types - Create an object type - Palantir
summary: Step-by-step guide for creating object types including metadata (display name, ID, description), backing datasource selection, property mapping, and key configuration. Primary way is via guided helper. All object types must have a title key and unique primary key. A single datasource can only back one object type.
relevantParts: Object type creation workflow in Ontology Manager
Required elements: title key, primary key, backing datasource
Single datasource per object type constraint

title: Object and link types - Object types - Metadata reference - Palantir
summary: Reference for object type metadata including display name, plural display name, description, ID, icon, color, groups, and status. Documents all configurable metadata fields that define how an object type appears and behaves across the platform.
relevantParts: Complete object type metadata field reference
Display configuration: name, icon, color
ID and status metadata

title: The Ontology system - Palantir
summary: Architectural overview of the Ontology as a multimodal system with dozens of components, grouped into Language (semantic types, kinetic actions, logic), Engine (read/write architecture), and Toolchain. The Engine provides SQL queries, real-time subscriptions, atomic transactions, batch mutations, and streaming.
relevantParts: Three-part architecture: Language, Engine, Toolchain
Engine capabilities: SQL, real-time, transactions, batch, streaming
Multimodal system with dozens of underlying components

title: Overview and getting started - Overview - Palantir
summary: The Ontology backend manages datasource feeding, schema definitions, querying/searching/aggregating objects, and orchestration of writes. Object Set Service (OSS) serves reads with filtering, aggregating, and loading. Object Storage V2 supports higher-scale Search Arounds (100K+ objects) and accurate aggregations via Spark-based query layer.
relevantParts: Backend responsibilities: datasource management, querying, write orchestration
Object Set Service (OSS) as query layer
Object Storage V2 Spark-based query execution

title: Object edits and materializations - How user edits are applied - Palantir
summary: When an Action is applied, edits are immediately applied to the index in object databases and periodically flushed to persistent storage as Foundry datasets. Transactionality guarantees avoid data correctness issues. Writeback datasets read edits and reflect the most up-to-date object state.
relevantParts: Action application flow: immediate index update, periodic flush
Transactionality guarantees for data correctness
Writeback datasets as persistent edit storage

title: Ontologies - Overview - Palantir
summary: An ontology stores ontological resources including object types, link types, action types, and interfaces. Ontologies can be private (single organization) or shared (multiple organizations). Mapped 1:1 with a space. When a new space is created, a corresponding ontology is simultaneously created.
relevantParts: Ontology as container for all ontological resources
Private vs shared ontology scoping
1:1 mapping between ontology and space

title: Ontologies - Proposals - Palantir
summary: Ontology proposals allow making changes on a branch, based on the main version of the Ontology. Every modified entity becomes a separate Task in the proposal for review. Changes are reviewed and approved before merging into the main Ontology. Supports creation, modification, and deletion of ontological resources.
relevantParts: Branch-based change management for Ontology modifications
Task-based review of individual entity changes
Approval workflow before merging to main Ontology

title: Foundry Branching - Overview - Palantir
summary: Foundry Branching enables developing and testing end-to-end workflows (code, data pipelines, Ontology) in isolation without disrupting production. Provides unified experience for modifications across multiple applications on a single branch. Branches are intended to be short-lived and small to avoid conflicts.
relevantParts: Unified branching across code, pipelines, and Ontology
Non-disruptive development and testing
Best practice: short-lived, small branches

title: Ontology Manager - Overview - Palantir
summary: Ontology Manager is the primary tool for building and maintaining the Ontology, from creating object types and action types to connecting data. Accessed via Workspace sidebar or URL. Supports guided step-by-step creation with metadata, backing datasource, property mappings, and key configuration.
relevantParts: Primary management tool for all Ontology configuration
Guided creation workflow for object types
Central hub for Ontology maintenance

title: Why create an Ontology? - Palantir
summary: Explains the strategic value of building an Ontology: shared source of truth for decision-making, data discovery, viewing local decisions in global context. Goes beyond data cataloging to define a robust foundation for end-user workflows with rich metadata and granular security.
relevantParts: Strategic value proposition of the Ontology
Beyond data cataloging: workflows, metadata, security
Shared source of truth across organization

title: Palantir Ontology
summary: Marketing/overview page for the Palantir Ontology platform. Presents the Ontology as mapping data to real-world objects (parts, machines, SKUs, patients, flights, oil wells) with relationships and business logic. All downstream applications read from and write to the same Ontology instead of scattered silos.
relevantParts: High-level positioning of Ontology as unified data layer
Real-world entity examples across industries
Single source of truth for all applications

title: Ontology-Oriented Software Development - Palantir Blog
summary: Introduces ontology-oriented development as a new paradigm where programmers abstract away internal implementation details and interact with business concepts. The OSDK provides a toolkit for building APIs in the language of the business. Aligns business users and engineers around business representation rather than underlying components.
relevantParts: Ontology-oriented development paradigm definition
OSDK as business-language API toolkit
Alignment of business users and engineers

title: Ontology: Finding meaning in data - Palantir Blog
summary: Foundational blog post explaining what Palantir means by 'ontology'. Data does not have inherent meaning; meaning is layered onto data by users. An ontology maps data to meaningful semantic concepts. Defines the nouns, verbs, and adjectives of an organization (e.g., Accounts, Transactions, Financial Products for a bank).
relevantParts: Philosophical foundation: meaning vs raw data
Ontology as mapping from data to semantic concepts
Nouns, verbs, adjectives metaphor for organizations

title: Connecting AI to Decisions with the Palantir Ontology - Palantir Blog
summary: The Ontology brings together data, logic, and action into a decision-centric model. Every decision comprises data (information), logic (evaluation), and action (execution). The Ontology integrates these into a scalable, dynamic foundation. LLMs and AIP agents can query and reason over the Ontology using natural language.
relevantParts: Decision-centric model: data + logic + action
AI/LLM integration with Ontology via natural language
Ontology as foundation for human + AI decision-making

title: Building with Palantir AIP: The Ontology Software Development Kit - Palantir Blog
summary: Details how the OSDK enables building external applications backed by the Foundry Ontology. The OSDK generates type-safe code from Ontology definitions, allowing developers to interact with business objects directly. Supports querying, filtering, and writing back to the Ontology from external applications.
relevantParts: OSDK for external application development
Type-safe code generation from Ontology definitions
Query, filter, and writeback capabilities

title: Understanding Palantir's Ontology: Semantic, Kinetic, and Dynamic Layers Explained
summary: Third-party analysis breaking down the three Ontology layers. Semantic layer defines the conceptual model (entities, relationships, properties). Kinetic layer operationalizes the model with data feeds, action types, and functions. Dynamic layer introduces business rules, access control, and lifecycle management.
relevantParts: Three-layer model: semantic, kinetic, dynamic
Clear delineation of each layer's responsibilities
Third-party perspective on Ontology architecture

title: Palantir Foundry: Ontology - Jimmy Wang - Medium
summary: Third-party tutorial-style overview of the Palantir Foundry Ontology covering core concepts, object types, properties, link types, and action types. Provides an accessible introduction to Ontology fundamentals from a practitioner's perspective.
relevantParts: Practitioner's perspective on Ontology fundamentals
Accessible introduction to core concepts
Third-party tutorial content

title: Foundational Ontologies in Palantir Foundry - Dorian Smiley - Medium
summary: Explores the concept of foundational ontologies in Palantir Foundry, discussing how to design base ontology patterns that can be extended for specific use cases. Covers ontology design patterns and best practices for building reusable foundations.
relevantParts: Foundational ontology design patterns
Reusable base ontology concepts
Best practices for ontology extensibility

title: Palantir's Ontology, Kimball's Star Schema, and Model-Driven Data Engineering: A Comparative View
summary: Comparative analysis of Palantir's Ontology approach against traditional data modeling paradigms like Kimball's star schema. Palantir's Ontology is described as a highly normalized, business-oriented abstraction layer that models data as business objects with relationships as first-class citizens.
relevantParts: Comparison with traditional data modeling approaches
Ontology as normalized business-oriented abstraction
Relationships as first-class citizens

title: Understanding the Palantir Ontology - PVM Blog
summary: Third-party educational resource providing an accessible overview of the Palantir Ontology concept, its components, and how it creates a unified data model for enterprises. Covers the digital twin concept and how the Ontology bridges data and decision-making.
relevantParts: Accessible third-party Ontology overview
Digital twin and unified data model concepts
Bridge between data and decision-making

title: Ontology and Pipeline Design Principles - Palantir Developer Community
summary: Community discussion on design principles for Ontology and data pipeline architecture. Covers best practices for structuring object types, managing data flow into the Ontology, and ensuring scalable, maintainable Ontology designs.
relevantParts: Community-sourced design principles
Pipeline-to-Ontology design guidance
Scalability and maintainability best practices

title: Polymorphic Interface with Unique Primary Keys - Best Practice? - Palantir Developer Community
summary: Community discussion about best practices for implementing polymorphic interfaces when object types have unique primary key requirements. Explores practical challenges of interface-based polymorphism in Ontology design.
relevantParts: Practical interface polymorphism challenges
Primary key uniqueness across interface implementations
Community-sourced best practice discussion

title: Introducing object type groups in the Ontology - Palantir Developer Community
summary: Announcement and discussion of object type groups feature for organizing and categorizing object types within the Ontology. Groups help users navigate large ontologies and discover relevant object types more efficiently.
relevantParts: Object type groups feature introduction
Organizational and discovery benefits
Large ontology management

title: Deep Dive: Creating Your First Ontology - Palantir Learn
summary: Official Palantir training course (60-90 minutes) providing hands-on guidance for building a first Ontology. Covers creating object types, defining properties, setting up link types, and configuring action types through guided exercises.
relevantParts: Official hands-on training course
End-to-end Ontology creation workflow
Covers all core primitives: objects, properties, links, actions

title: List Object Types - API Reference - Palantir
summary: API reference for listing object types in an Ontology. Returns object type definitions with metadata including display names, property schemas, primary keys, and descriptions. Part of the Ontology Resources API.
relevantParts: REST API for enumerating object types
Object type definition structure in API responses
Property schemas and metadata in API format

title: Get Object Type - API Reference - Palantir
summary: V2 API reference for retrieving a specific object type definition by its API name. Returns property map, display names, primary key, description, and other metadata. Demonstrates the API representation of object type schemas.
relevantParts: V2 API for individual object type retrieval
API name-based lookup
Complete object type schema in response

title: Aggregate Object Set - API Reference - Palantir
summary: V2 API for aggregating objects in an ObjectSet. Supports aggregation functions: count, min, max, sum, average. Takes an input object set definition and runs aggregating functions on specified properties. Requires api:ontologies-read OAuth scope.
relevantParts: ObjectSet aggregation API
Aggregation functions: count, min, max, sum, avg
OAuth scope requirements

title: Query basics - API Reference - Palantir
summary: API reference for query fundamentals in the Ontology. A query type represents logic that can be run in the Ontology. Covers query execution, parameter passing, and result handling through the API gateway.
relevantParts: Query API fundamentals
Query type definition and execution
API gateway integration for queries

title: Ontologies - Usage - Ontology query compute - Palantir
summary: The Ontology is a data backend that maps file-based data to organization-centric objects and serves high-speed queries for exploration, analysis, operational editing, scenario analysis, and more. Data is stored in multi-modal storage backends that can be flexibly queried in a single request.
relevantParts: Ontology as high-speed query backend
Multi-modal storage backends
Flexible single-request querying across backends

title: Time series - Sensor object types use case - Create sensor object types with Ontology Manager - Palantir
summary: Guide for creating sensor object types in the Ontology, a pattern where sensor objects hold time series data linked to parent/root objects. Provides flexibility for per-object time series data. Covers two approaches: direct time series properties (TSPs) on objects vs linked sensor object types.
relevantParts: Sensor object type pattern for time series data
Two time series approaches: direct TSP vs linked sensors
Per-object time series flexibility

title: Object and link types - Properties - Mandatory control properties - Palantir
summary: Mandatory control properties are system-level properties that must always have values and cannot be null. They can be configured to allow empty arrays. These properties ensure critical metadata is always present on object instances.
relevantParts: Mandatory control properties as system-enforced fields
Non-nullable constraint
Empty array allowance configuration

title: Object and link types - Value types - Value type versions - Palantir
summary: Value types support versioning to manage evolution of semantic type definitions over time. Versions allow updating constraints, metadata, and behavior while maintaining backward compatibility with existing property definitions.
relevantParts: Value type versioning mechanism
Evolution of semantic type definitions
Backward compatibility management

title: Ontologies - Shared ontologies - Palantir
summary: A shared ontology enables resources from multiple organizations to be used in a workflow, whereas a private ontology contains resources from a single organization. Shared ontologies allow safe cross-organization collaboration on data and workflows.
relevantParts: Cross-organization ontology sharing
Shared vs private ontology distinction
Multi-organization workflow enablement

title: Palantir Foundry 101 - Unit8
summary: Third-party introductory guide to Palantir Foundry covering the platform's core components including the Ontology. Provides an outside perspective on how Foundry's data integration, transformation, and Ontology layers work together for enterprise use cases.
relevantParts: Third-party introduction to Foundry and Ontology
Platform component integration overview
Enterprise use case perspective

title: Object permissioning - Multi-datasource object types (MDOs) - Palantir
summary: Multi-datasource object types (MDOs) are backed by multiple datasources. Only column-wise MDOs are supported (not row-wise). A specific property must come from one datasource only, except the primary key which must exist in every datasource for joining. Only datasets or restricted views can be used.
relevantParts: Multi-datasource object type pattern
Column-wise composition constraint
Primary key as join key across datasources

title: Action types - Action log - Palantir
summary: The Action Log provides audit trail for all actions taken in the Ontology, tracking who made what changes and when. Supports accountability and compliance requirements by maintaining a complete history of Ontology modifications.
relevantParts: Audit trail for Ontology actions
Change tracking: who, what, when
Compliance and accountability support

title: Geospatial and geotemporal - Use geospatial data in the Ontology - Palantir
summary: Guide for integrating geospatial data into the Ontology using geopoint and geoshape property types. Supports point, line, and polygon geometries. Objects can have georectified images attached with bounds. Integrates with Map application for visualization.
relevantParts: Geospatial property types: geopoint, geoshape
Point, line, polygon geometry support
Georectified image attachment capability

title: Media sets (unstructured data) - Using media in the Ontology - Palantir
summary: Media reference object properties allow efficient display of media in Ontology-based applications. Provides optimizations like faster previews in Workshop/Object Explorer and tiling for geospatial imagery in Map. Bridges unstructured media data with structured Ontology objects.
relevantParts: Media reference properties for unstructured data
Application-level optimizations for media display
Integration of unstructured media with Ontology objects

title: GitHub - palantir/foundry-platform-python: The official Python SDK for the Foundry API
summary: Official Python SDK for interacting with the Foundry API, including Ontology operations. Provides programmatic access to object types, properties, links, actions, and queries from Python applications.
relevantParts: Official Python SDK for Foundry/Ontology API
Programmatic access to all Ontology operations
Open source reference implementation

title: Ontology SDK - OSDK references - Unsupported types for OSDK - Palantir
summary: Documents which Ontology types are not yet supported by the OSDK, providing insight into the type system boundaries. Helps understand which Ontology primitives can and cannot be accessed through generated SDK code.
relevantParts: OSDK type support limitations
Understanding type system boundaries
Gap analysis for SDK coverage

title: Object and link types - Properties - Metadata reference - Palantir
summary: Complete reference for property metadata fields including base type, render hints, visibility, searchable flag, sortable flag, and other configuration options. Documents all configurable aspects of property definitions in the Ontology.
relevantParts: Complete property metadata field reference
All configurable property attributes
Render hints, visibility, searchable configuration

title: Object and link types - Link types - Metadata reference - Palantir
summary: Reference for link type metadata including ID, display names (bidirectional), cardinality, and other configuration. Each side of a link type has its own display name. The link type ID is used for referencing in application configuration.
relevantParts: Complete link type metadata reference
Bidirectional display name system
Link type ID for application referencing
